channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: SpaceX’s IPO Could Turn 4,400 Employees Into Millionaires
item.description: While Elon Musk may soon become a trillionaire, his rocket company’s market debut is set to the change the lives of its current and former employees, too.
item.dc:creator: Kirsten Grind

item.title: Antonio Gracias, Elon Musk’s Friend, Is Set to Reap Billions From SpaceX’s IPO
item.description: Antonio Gracias, an investor who has built a $65 billion stake in the company, exemplifies the economic upside of being Mr. Musk’s ally.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Jim Vondruska for The New York Times
item.media:description: Antonio Gracias, left, and Elon Musk at a political event in Green Bay, Wis., last spring. Mr. Gracias and his private equity firm control a $65 billion stake in SpaceX.

item.title: Investors Feed A.I. Firms’ Voracious Appetite for New Money
item.description: In the race to dominate the artificial intelligence industry, companies like SpaceX and Alphabet are borrowing cash and raising equity from investors at the fastest pace in decades.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison, Lauren Hirsch and Maureen Farrell
item.media:description: The amount of cash needed for A.I. development is thought to be so enormous that already cash-rich companies are looking for more money.

item.title: SpaceX IPO: What You Need to Know
item.description: As the company prepares for its stock market debut, here’s what the initial public offering means for banks, Elon Musk and everyday investors.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shares of SpaceX are expected to start trading on Friday.

item.title: How Quiet Oman Landed Itself in Trump’s Cross Hairs
item.description: As the Iran war drags on, Oman — a U.S. ally and mediator with Iran — has found itself at odds with the Trump administration and some of its own neighbors.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fishing in Muscat, Oman, in February, before the war between the United States, Israel and Iran started.

item.title: Inside Trump’s White House, the Epstein Files Caused a Freakout
item.description: The president’s top advisers gathered in a series of Situation Room meetings as they struggled to contain a scandal engulfing Donald Trump himself.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan

item.title: The White House Panic Over the Epstein Files: Six Takeaways From the New York Times Investigation
item.description: Senior officials clashed in a series of meetings as they struggled to manage a crisis over the president’s refusal to release the documents.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.media:description: Protesters at the White House last July.

item.title: Bill Gates Says Epstein Tried To Use His Extramarital Affairs Against Him
item.description: The billionaire philanthropist testified on Wednesday in a closed-door congressional hearing about the Justice Department’s investigation of Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: Revelations about Bill Gates’s connection to Jeffrey Epstein came to light after Mr. Epstein was arrested in 2019.

item.title: The Big Questions About Jeffrey Epstein: What The Times Has Learned
item.description: Here is what we know about the sexual predator whose secrets spurred an international reckoning over money, power and complicity. Check back for updates.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Strict Security Around MSG Is Back for Knicks Game 4
item.description: Several blocks around Madison Square Garden will be closed to most people Wednesday afternoon. The city approved a ticketed watch party, though it was unclear whether it would take place.
item.dc:creator: Caitlyn Freeman and Andy Newman
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Knicks face the San Antonio Spurs in the N.B.A. finals. Fan celebrations outside Madison Square Garden have at times been rowdy or violent.

item.title: New York Is Buzzing With Watch Parties for the Knicks’ NBA Finals Games
item.description: In bodegas and parks, in bars and pizzerias, the unofficial watch party has taken over New York.
item.dc:creator: David Waldstein and The New York Times

item.title: Will Trump’s Repairs to Washington D.C.’s Reflecting Pool Work?
item.description: President Trump has said that construction is complete. Now comes the real test. Will the longstanding problems finally be solved?
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Says He’s ‘Not Looking to Renew’ Canada-Mexico Trade Deal
item.description: The president’s comments come at a crucial moment in talks among the three countries over the renewal of their free-trade agreement.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff, Ana Swanson and Tony Romm
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney with President Trump at the Group of 7 summit last year in Kananaskis, Canada.

item.title: They Tried To Catch a Child Predator on a Livestream. They Trapped Themselves Instead.
item.description: What happened when livestreaming vigilantes ambushed an innocent man.
item.dc:creator: Kashmir Hill and Aric Toler

item.title: A Twist in Ukraine’s Drone Campaign Is ‘Really Hurting the Russians’
item.description: Midrange attacks, using upgraded drones that Ukraine produces in huge numbers, are causing fuel shortages and complicating troop rotations.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soldiers preparing to launch drones at targets in Russia from an undisclosed location in Ukraine last month.

item.title: Visiting a Soldier’s Funeral in Ukraine
item.description: The New York Times reporter Kim Barker visited a church in Lviv, Ukraine, that holds military funerals at the same time each day.
item.dc:creator: Kim Barker, Whitney Shefte, Michael Anthony Adams, Oleksandra Mykolyshyn, Sutton Raphael and Rebecca Suner

item.title: Senior Russian Military Official Is Killed in Car Explosion Near Moscow
item.description: The death of the officer appeared to add to a string of targeted assassinations of high-profile opponents of Ukraine inside Russia.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: Wreckage after an explosion in the city of Balashikha, east of Moscow, on Tuesday.

item.title: Why Complete Election Results in Maine Could Take More Than a Week
item.description: Winners in the Republican and Democratic primary races for governor and in a Democratic congressional primary have not yet been determined.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voting in Belfast, Maine, on Tuesday. Arielle Greenberg, left, wore a hand-sewn skirt that she said she made from an American flag.

item.title: Graham Platner’s Victory in Maine Senate Primary Gives Democrats Hope and Fears
item.description: Graham Platner’s primary victory sets up a high-stakes contest between an insurgent progressive with political baggage and a battle-tested but vulnerable Republican senator.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner will face Senator Susan Collins, a moderate Republican, in a general election in Maine that is seen as central to the fight for Senate control.

item.title: Inflation Accelerates to Fastest Pace in 3 Years as Energy Prices Bite
item.description: Companies appear hesitant to pass those price increases on to weary consumers, whose wages aren’t keeping up.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: Trump Shrugs Off High Prices Amid Iran War and Says ‘I Love the Inflation’
item.description: As inflation outpaces workers’ wages, the Trump administration insists that its agenda is working.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Speaking at a bill signing on Wednesday, President Trump predicted that inflation would recede quickly, once the Iran conflict concludes.“ It’s going to come down like a rock.”

item.title: Why the Real A.I. Threat Is in the Back Office
item.description: As artificial intelligence spreads, millions of middle-class jobs in human resources, billing and payroll could be at risk. Most are held by women.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman

item.title: A.I. Chatbot Helps a $100 Thrift Store Painting Sell for Over $250,000
item.description: When a son got curious about the origins of a painting his mother bought at a secondhand shop decades ago, Google Gemini had some intriguing thoughts.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez

item.title: Night of Violence Grips Belfast After Stabbing Attack
item.description: Cars and a bus were set on fire and families were driven from their homes after a stabbing caused tensions to spill onto the streets.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Strife erupted in Belfast, Northern Ireland, on Tuesday amid anger over a stabbing attributed to a Sudanese man.

item.title: Violence in Belfast After Brutal Stabbing Attack: What to Know
item.description: There was unrest in Belfast and elsewhere after video of a brutal stabbing circulated online, amplified by far-right activists.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: The aftermath of overnight riots in Belfast, Northern Ireland, on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Officials Say ICE Won’t Raid World Cup Games, but Fans Are Worried
item.description: Federal agents are there for security, not immigration enforcement, officials say. But trust is fragile after the administration’s recent crackdowns.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango and Jesus Jiménez
item.media:description: Federal agents outside of Dodger Stadium in Los Angeles last June during the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement surge, which has stirred anxiety.

item.title: 7 Revolutionary-Era Bars That Are as Old as America
item.description: Taverns were important incubators of revolutionary fervor where patriots gathered to plot and gossip. Here are a few where you can still taste the spirit of 1776.
item.dc:creator: Liza Weisstuch and James Estrin

item.title: California’s Excuses Are Damaging Faith in Government
item.description: Taking a week to count votes is doing it wrong.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Illustration by Rebecca Chew/The New York Times

item.title: A Different Kind of ‘Values Voter’ Will Power the 2026 Midterms
item.description: A new politics of morality is rising in the Democratic Party.
item.dc:creator: E.J. Dionne Jr.

item.title: Are You Traveling to the U.S. for the 2026 World Cup? We’d Like to Hear From You.
item.description: Tell us about your experience buying tickets, finding a place to stay, making travel arrangements and, if you’re coming to the U.S., dealing with entry requirements.
item.dc:creator: Danial Adkison
item.media:description: MetLife Stadium in New Jersey will be hosting eight World Cup matches.

item.title: Duane Michals, Photographer With Stories to Tell, Dies at 94
item.description: A self-taught artist, he brought narrative to modern photography with sequences of staged black-and-white images, often accompanied by wry or lyrical captions.
item.dc:creator: Richard B. Woodward
